Q1: I tried to keep a pretty consistent study routine, honestly. I'd block out time most days, usually in the mornings when my apartment is quiet, and I'd go through my course materials systematically. I spent around 26 hours a week studying, which felt like a lot, but I always made sure to complete what was assigned. My routine was really about checking boxes and making sure I didn't fall behind on submissions.
Q2: I definitely tried to stick to regular times because that's just how I function best. Mornings were my thing, and I'd try to listen to lectures or recorded materials during that time since I learn better by hearing things. Sometimes deadlines would push me to adjust, but mostly I kept to my schedule. The predictability helped me feel less anxious about everything.
Q3: Usually I logged in to check if there were new assignments posted or to see if any deadlines were coming up. I also wanted to make sure I hadn't missed any announcements from the instructor. It became almost automatic, like checking my email first thing. I logged in pretty frequently, probably out of habit more than anything else.
Q4: Yeah, logging into the LMS definitely became part of my daily routine after the first few weeks. I think it happened because I was worried about missing something important, so I just built it into my morning. It was like brushing my teeth—I didn't really think about it, I just did it. The consistency helped me stay on top of submissions even when I was struggling with the actual content.
Q5: The video lectures were the most useful for me because I'm an auditory learner. I could listen to them multiple times if something didn't click the first time, and I watched a lot of video content. The discussion boards were also helpful when other students explained things in different ways. Hearing different perspectives sometimes made concepts clearer than the original lecture.
Q6: I honestly didn't use the textbook resources much because I don't have the money to buy all the recommended materials, and the library copies were always checked out. Some of the supplementary readings I skipped because they felt overwhelming when I was already struggling to understand the main content. I focused on what was required and let the extras slide.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to an assignment or if I could tell it would help me on the exam. I also valued things I could listen to rather than read because that's how I process information best. If something felt like busy work or I couldn't see the point, I'd do it quickly just to complete it but not really engage deeply.
Q8: Optional activities always felt like a luxury I couldn't afford, honestly. With limited resources and time, I had to prioritize what was required. Required activities got my full attention because they affected my grade, while optional ones I'd only do if I had extra time, which was rare. It's not that I didn't want to do them; I just had to be strategic.
Q9: Absolutely, I did a lot of studying offline. I'd download lecture recordings and listen to them on my phone while commuting or doing chores. I couldn't always afford to use data, so I'd download things when I had wifi and study them later. I also took handwritten notes while listening because writing helps me remember what I hear.
Q10: There were many times I was studying but the LMS wouldn't show it. Like when I'd replay downloaded lectures on my phone or review my handwritten notes for hours. I'd also talk through concepts out loud to myself, which really helps me understand things, but obviously that doesn't show up anywhere. My real study time was way more than what the system could track.
Q11: Yes, all the time. I'd access a video lecture once online, download it, and then listen to it maybe three or four more times offline. The LMS would only show that one initial view, but I was actually engaging with that material repeatedly. It was the most efficient way for me to study given my situation with limited internet access at home sometimes.
Q12: There were definitely harder moments, especially when I couldn't understand the material no matter how many times I listened to it. My attendance in live sessions was really low because of my work schedule and other responsibilities. When I'd get a bad grade back, it was discouraging even though I'd put in so much effort. I started feeling like maybe I just wasn't getting it.
Q13: Competing responsibilities definitely affected me. I have limited resources, so I work part-time, and that ate into my study time and my ability to attend live sessions. Fatigue was real—sometimes I'd log in and watch videos but realize I wasn't actually absorbing anything. The workload from having 15 online courses was overwhelming at times.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd be watching a video and realize I'd zoned out completely. I was technically logged in and the video was playing, but my mind was elsewhere, worrying about bills or just exhausted from work. Those moments felt frustrating because I was trying to engage but my brain just wasn't cooperating. The activity was there but the learning wasn't.
Q15: I rarely postponed assignments because completing them was something I could control, unlike understanding the material. But I did avoid exam preparation sometimes because it stressed me out knowing I might not do well no matter how much I studied. I'd tell myself I'd review later, then run out of time. It was easier to complete assignments than to face the exams.
Q16: Deadlines were my anchor, honestly. They kept me moving forward and logging in regularly. I'd organize my whole week around when things were due. Without deadlines, I probably would have fallen apart. But sometimes multiple deadlines at once meant I was just rushing to submit rather than really learning the material deeply.
Q17: Feedback was hit or miss for me. When I got positive feedback, it motivated me to keep going. But when I got poor exam scores back, it was confusing because I felt like I'd done the work. The CBT session I had helped me process some of that disappointment and reframe how I thought about setbacks. It made me realize I needed different strategies, not just more effort.
Q18: The course structure was pretty rigid, which actually worked for me because I like routine. Having clear deadlines and a predictable schedule helped me plan my week. But the structure didn't account for different learning styles or resource levels. I felt like I was keeping up with the pace but not actually mastering the content.
Q19: The video content made engagement easier because I could listen and re-listen. But the exams were harder because they required a different kind of understanding than just completing assignments. The low attendance requirement meant I could technically pass that part, but missing live sessions meant missing real-time explanations. That made things harder for me.
Q20: I think I might be an example of that, actually. I was highly active in the LMS—logging in constantly, watching videos, completing tasks—but my exam scores didn't reflect that effort. Sometimes you can go through all the motions and still not grasp the material deeply enough for exams. Activity doesn't always equal understanding, which was a hard lesson for me.
Q21: I can see how that happens. Some people are just naturally better at grasping concepts quickly or they have better resources like tutors or study groups. They might not need to spend as much time in the LMS because they're efficient learners. For me, I needed more time and still struggled, so I can't really relate to that experience personally.
Q22: Course logs can show consistency and effort—like how often you log in or complete tasks. But they miss the quality of engagement and whether you're actually learning. They can't see me listening to downloaded lectures on the bus or talking through problems out loud. They also can't measure confusion or how hard you're trying despite struggling.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely trying to understand the material, not just going through the motions. It means showing up mentally even when it's hard and caring about whether you're learning. For me, I was engaged in terms of effort and routine, but I realize now that engagement also needs to lead to understanding, which I struggled with despite trying hard.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I studied offline or how many times I replayed lectures on my phone. It wouldn't show the emotional toll of failing despite completing everything or the surprise I felt when my grades didn't match my effort. It also wouldn't capture the CBT session that helped me cope or the financial stress that affected my focus. There's a whole person behind those numbers.
